preFix.title: Lombardic capitals
preFix.subtitle: The decorated versals of medieval illuminated manuscripts, built up from multiple broad-edge strokes
preFix.excerpt: Lombardic capitals are the large ornamental letters used at the opening of chapters and sections in medieval manuscripts. Built up from two or three broad-edge strokes, they carry hairline serifs, internal diamond-shaped counter spaces, and optional geometric infill.
preFix.sourceNotes: Edward Johnston, Writing and Illuminating and Lettering (1906), chapter on built-up versals and the principles of letter construction. Internet Archive: https://archive.org/details/writingillumina00john. Johnston's analysis of built-up letter construction and his alphabet of Roman versals forms the foundation of modern Lombardic instruction.

content.text: A
versal
is not written in one pass. It is built: drawn as a skeleton, then the two outer edges of each stroke are drawn separately, and the space between them is filled in. This
built-up stroke

method produces the slight biconvex swell in each stroke that distinguishes the Lombardic family from a flat-edged broad-pen letter. Edward Johnston called this swell "the foundation of the beauty of the letter".
What you need

items.name: Mitchell Roundhand nib, 3 (medium, for the finished fills)
Light box, A4 (optional for guide-line transfer)

content.text: Proportions and nib angle

Lombardic capitals are tall: the capital height is typically seven nib widths with a Mitchell 0 nib. Rule two guide lines: baseline and cap height. The nib angle for the structural strokes is 30 degrees from the baseline. The angle is steeper than for Roman inscriptional work and produces the slight thick-to-thin transition in straight strokes.
Building the letter C
C is the clearest letter to learn first because the built-up stroke method is visible at every stage.
Draw the left outer edge of the letter in pencil: a curved arc from the upper serif down to the lower serif, swelling outward at the left. This is the spine of the C.
Draw the right inner edge of the curved stroke parallel to the first, leaving a stroke width of roughly 1.5 nib widths between the two curves.

Add the serif wedges at the head and tail: each is a short downward stroke that flares out to a flat baseline, then turns to meet the main stroke. The classic Lombardic serif is a pointed entry with a blunt horizontal foot.
Fill the stroke body with ink using the broad nib, keeping the fill slightly inside the pencil outline. The biconvex swell should be visible: the stroke is wider at its centre than at its ends.
When dry, erase the pencil guidelines. The
counter
of the C should be a roughly circular open space.
Stroke order for the full alphabet

The Lombardic alphabet shares a consistent stroke order across all letters. Curved letters (C, G, O, Q, D) build from the main arc first, then the counter. Straight letters (I, L, T, E, F) build the main vertical first, then horizontal arms. Letters with diagonals (A, K, M, N, V, W, X) draw the diagonals as separate built-up strokes, then the connecting hairlines.

Many Lombardic letters carry hairlines inside them. A small curved stroke sits inside the bowl of the O. A diamond spine runs inside the counter of the C. Add these last, with the finest nib you have, once the main body strokes are dry.
Common mistakes

items.fix: Draw the outline edges as a lens shape, slightly wider at the centre than at head and tail. This is the built-up stroke technique; flat outlines produce a different letter family.
items.cause: The two outline edges were drawn as parallel lines rather than slightly curving inward at the ends.
items.symptom: The strokes are flat-sided instead of biconvex

items.fix: Use a drier ink consistency and a sized paper (cartridge 220 gsm or hot-press watercolour paper). Erase the pencil first and redraw the outline in a faint line if needed.
items.cause: The ink is too wet or the paper is too absorbent.
items.symptom: The ink fill bleeds outside the pencil outline

tricolon: tricolon (three parallel items) — two adjectives almost always beats three
